conclusion_paragraphs: Based on our forensic analysis of 96 documented findings, the property at 500 S Main St, El Dorado Springs, MO 64744, USA sustained confirmed storm damage from the April 26, 2026 hail event. Chalk testing performed across multiple runs of R-panel metal roofing consistently revealed circular gap patterns in chalk lines, documenting widespread hail impact dents across both slopes of the metal roof system. Beyond the metal panel damage, the inspection identified coating disruption with bare metal exposure at impact points, chimney counter-flashing separation from the stucco substrate creating active water intrusion pathways, and code-noncompliant wall-to-roof flashing conditions at the brick elevation — all of which compound the storm's effect on the property's weathertight integrity.

The confirmed hail damage to the architectural laminated comp shingle sections and the R-panel metal roof system, combined with multiple documented code violations under IBC 1503.2, IRC R903.2.1, IRC R905.2.8.3, IRC R903.2, and IMC 304.10, establishes that spot repairs are insufficient to restore this property to a code-compliant, weathertight condition. The metal panels exhibit coating disruption and bare metal exposure at hail impact points, creating progressive corrosion vulnerability that cannot be addressed through localized repair — panel-by-panel replacement of a continuous metal roof system is neither practical nor warrantable. Additionally, the presence of two distinct roofing systems on one structure (metal panels and asphalt shingles) means that partial scope cannot achieve material compatibility, uniform performance, or manufacturer warranty coverage across the completed assembly.

Based on the documented damage, applicable Missouri-adopted IRC building code requirements, and industry standards, our analysis identifies the need for full replacement of both the R-panel metal roof system and the asphalt shingle roof sections, along with remediation of all associated flashing, ridge cap, pipe boot, and HVAC curb components. The forensic evidence — including chalk-tested hail impacts across the entire metal roof surface, chimney counter-flashing failures, non-code-compliant step and counter-flashing at wall intersections, and functionally damaged shingle sections — supports a comprehensive roof system replacement scope to restore the property at 500 S Main St, El Dorado Springs, MO 64744, USA to pre-loss, code-compliant condition.

This forensic determination was developed in accordance with HAAG Engineering certified hail impact identification methodology, NRCA roofing inspection guidelines, and the International Residential Code (IRC) as adopted in Missouri. Chalk testing protocols, damage pattern distribution analysis, and code compliance evaluation collectively form the evidentiary basis for the scope recommendation outlined above.

The building's metal siding panels and window screens present soft metal surfaces susceptible to hail damage. Photo 89 shows hail damage evidence on window screen. executive_summary: On April 26, 2026, a hail-producing storm system impacted the property located at 500 S Main St, El Dorado Springs, MO 64744, USA. A comprehensive forensic inspection was conducted and documented across 97 photographs, encompassing two roofing systems present on the property: standing seam/R-panel metal roofing on the main structure and architectural laminated composition shingles on the lower structure. The inspection also evaluated associated components including ridge caps, roof penetration flashings, pipe boots, attic vents, and a rooftop HVAC curb assembly.

Chalk testing performed across the metal roof panels confirmed widespread hail impact damage, with circular gaps in chalk lines documenting dent locations throughout the flat pan areas of multiple panels. Impact points exhibit coating disruption, indentation of the metal substrate, and oxidation splatter patterns consistent with hail strike signatures from the reported storm event. On the shingled roof section, inspector-marked hail impacts were documented adjacent to penetrations, and functional damage was observed where shingle mat exposure and granule displacement were present at butt edges — damage patterns characteristic of hail-induced fracture on architectural laminated shingles.

The pervasive distribution of confirmed hail impacts across the metal roof system has compromised the protective galvalume coating at numerous points, rendering spot repair insufficient to restore weathertight integrity or arrest progressive corrosion at each impact site. On the shingled section, the combination of widespread hail-induced granule loss and mat fracturing precludes selective shingle replacement due to material discontinuity, color-lot unavailability, and the inability to achieve a code-compliant weather-resistant barrier through patchwork repairs under IRC Section R905.2. Full replacement of both roofing systems is the technically warranted repair methodology to return the property to its pre-loss, code-compliant condition.

photo_integrity.summary: All 20 photos pass integrity review. Images show a legitimate commercial/institutional building roof inspection covering both standing seam metal and asphalt shingle roof sections. Chalk marks on metal panels are consistent with standard hail damage testing methodology (sideways chalk swipe revealing impact dents). Numbered chalk notations on shingle penetrations are standard inspector documentation for feature counting. Wind-damaged shingles appear consistent with natural storm damage patterns. Inspector presence, shadows, and lighting are consistent across photos. No evidence of staged damage, digital manipulation, photo recycling, or misrepresentation detected.
